1	# GDPR Right to Erasure Orchestration Platform
2	
3	[![made-with-python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Made%20with-Python-1f425f.svg)](https://www.python.org/)
4	[![prek](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/j178/prek/master/docs/assets/badge-v0.json)](https://github.com/j178/prek)
5	[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)
6	[![pipeline status](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/pipeline.svg)](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/commits/master)
7	![coverage](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/coverage.svg?job=test)
8	
9	This project was originally created to replace the
10	[Tines](https://www.tines.com/) based Right to Erasure Orchestrator.
11	
12	## Project Goals
13	
14	The primary goal of this project is to replace the existing Tines based
15	orchestrator for subject erasure requests in regards to the GDPR legislation.
16	
17	The main problems being solved are as followed:
18	
19	- The costs related to the use of Tines
20	- The use of Jira as a point of integration and state storage
21	
22	This project also moves the ownership of the orchestration into the Data
23	Engineering team.
24	
25	## API
26	
27	The orchestrator exposes a private REST API (deployed via AWS API Gateway and
28	restricted to the configured VPC endpoint). All requests must include the
29	`x-api-key` header.
30	
31	### `POST /erasure-request`
32	
33	Creates a new erasure request and queues it for processing.
34	
35	```sh
36	curl -k -sS -X POST \
37	  'https://omqlg03jma.execute-api.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/prod/erasure-request' \
38	  -H 'x-api-key: <api-key>' \
39	  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
40	  -d '{
41	    "id": "<unique-id>",
42	    "source": "onetrust",
43	    "date_issued": "2026-01-01T12:00:00+00:00",
44	    "erasure_details": { "email": "user@example.com" }
45	  }'
46	```
47	
48	Required fields: `id`, `source`, `date_issued` (ISO-8601), `erasure_details`
49	(object with keys from `email`, `first-name`, `last-name`, `postcode`,
50	`phone-number`, `rfl`, `last-order`). Optional: `site_ids` (list of integers).
51	
52	| Status | Meaning |
53	|--------|---------|
54	| `200` | Request accepted; body is `{"id": "<id>"}` |
55	| `400` | Invalid or malformed request body |
56	| `409` | A request with this `id` already exists |
57	| `500` | Internal error |
58	
59	### `GET /status/{id}`
60	
61	Returns the audit log entries for an erasure request, ordered chronologically
62	(oldest first). Backed by `reporter_lambda`, which reads from DynamoDB and
63	falls back to the materialised S3 audit log.
64	
65	```sh
66	curl -k -sS -G \
67	  'https://omqlg03jma.execute-api.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/prod/status/<id>' \
68	  -H 'x-api-key: <api-key>'
69	```
70	
71	A `200` response has the form:
72	
73	```json
74	{
75	  "id": "<id>",
76	  "entries": [
77	    { "id": "<id>", "timestamp": "<iso-8601>", "message": "<text>" }
78	  ]
79	}
80	```
81	
82	The endpoint returns `404` if no audit entries exist for the given id, and
83	`400` if the id path parameter is missing or blank.
84	
85	## Audit log lifecycle
86	
87	Audit log entries are written to DynamoDB throughout request processing, keyed
88	by `(erasure_id, timestamp_ms)`.
89	
90	Once a request is successfully processed and reaches the terminal `done` state,
91	the process Lambda archives the complete audit log to S3 as a single gzipped
92	JSON Lines object at `s3://{AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET}/{AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX}/{id}.jsonl.gz`,
93	then deletes the DynamoDB entries for that id.
94	
95	The `GET /status/{id}` endpoint reads from DynamoDB first and falls back to the
96	S3 archive automatically, so reads are seamless before and after archival.
97	
98	### Environment variables (process Lambda)
99	
100	| Variable | Purpose |
101	|---|---|
102	| `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` | Request storage table |
103	| `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` | GSI name for state-based queries |
104	| `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE` | Audit log DynamoDB table |
105	| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET` | S3 bucket for materialised audit log archives |
106	| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX` | Key prefix for archive objects within the bucket |
107	
108	All five are wired automatically in SAM deployments via `template.yaml`
109	parameters. When running locally, set them in a `.env` file at the project
110	root.
111	
112	## Project Details
113	
114	This project is built using Python 3.13+ and uses
115	[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) as a package manager and build tool.
116	
117	> [!note]
118	> If you need help setting up your development environment, please refer to
119	> [docs/setup-local-dev.md](/docs/setup-local-dev.md).
120	
121	## Development
122	
123	After cloning this repository you should run:
124	
125	```sh
126	uv sync --all-groups
127	uv run prek install
128	```
129	
130	This should create a virtual environment under `.venv` in the local directory,
131	download all dependencies, and install a Git pre-commit hook.
132	
133	Generally speaking, if you want to run a normal Python command you should
134	prefix it with `uv run`.
135	
136	### Testing
137	
138	This project uses `pytest` for unit testing. These should be run using the
139	following command:
140	
141	```sh
142	uv run pytest
143	```
144	
145	`pytest-cov` is used as an extension that can help you see how much coverage
146	the tests are actually giving. You can run this as follows:
147	
148	```sh
149	uv run pytest --cov=dns_de_rte_orchestrator
150	```
151	
152	There isn't a set test coverage percentage code should have, but key files
153	should generally have more coverage than others.
154	
155	### Structure
156	
157	The key entry points for AWS Lambda are:
158	
159	- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`
160	- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py`
161	
162	The storage logic sits primarily in the `RequestRepository` parent class and its
163	implementations in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py`.
164	
165	The business logic sits in the `RequestProcessor` class in
166	`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`.
167	
168	### Managing Dependencies
169	
170	`pip` commands should not be used to manage dependencies, instead you should
171	use commands such as:
172	
173	```sh
174	# To add a library dependency
175	uv add library
176	# To add a development dependency only
177	uv add --dev pytest
178	# To remove a dependency
179	uv remove library
180	```
181	
182	This should update the `uv.lock` file, but if in doubt you can run `uv lock`
183	to regenerate it.
184	
185	You can attempt to upgrade packages using the following `uv` command:
186	
187	```sh
188	uv lock --upgrade-package library
189	```
190	
191	## Deployment
192	
193	The code in this project is deployed as 2 separate AWS Lambdas using [AWS
194	SAM](https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/).
195	
196	The versions of Python used during development, testing, and deployment should
197	all match as closely as possible.
198	
199	GitLab runners are used to execute tests and deployments. The configuration for
200	this is managed in `.gitlab-ci.yml`.
201	
202	### SAM
203	
204	AWS SAM uses a custom `Makefile` for building the package that gets uploaded to
205	AWS. This `Makefile` should not be invoked manually. The gist of it is:
206	
207	- It generates a `requirements.txt` with all the non-dev dependencies based on
208	  the `uv.lock` file
209	- It installs all these requirements into the artifact directory
210	- It copies the project `src/` code into the artifact directory
211	- It deletes the `requirements.txt` file
212	
213	The reason for the custom process is to reduce the size of the uploaded
214	artifact and to keep it replicable across different AWS SAM versions.
215	
216	> [!note]
217	> `sam local invoke` has problems when combined with the
218	> [ServerlessAccessManager](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/platform-as-a-service/serverless-applications/serverless-access-manager/)
219	> that the DSO team provide for us to use. You'll need to comment it out and
220	> any lines that reference it in order to get `sam local invoke` to work.
221	
222	To do a manual deployment you'll need to do the following:
223	
224	```sh
225	# First run the build process, this will execute the Makefile
226	sam build
227	# Next deploy
228	sam deploy \
229	  --config-file ../../samconfig.toml \
230	  --config-env dev \
231	  --template-file .aws-sam/build/template.yaml
232	```
233	
234	Note that you'll need to point at the `samconfig.toml` in the project root. By
235	default AWS SAM will try and search the same directory as the template file for
236	the `samconfig.toml`.
237	
238	## Code Quality Tools
239	
240	This project makes use of several tools to ensure consistent code quality.
241	
242	You can run these tools locally alongside `pytest` by running:
243	
244	```sh
245	./local-ci.sh
246	```
247	
248	### prek
249	
250	[prek](https://prek.j178.dev/) is a replacement for the
251	[pre-commit](https://pre-commit.com/) framework. Both allow you to run scripts
252	and checks before a Git commit is created.
253	
254	`prek` is primarily used to run the other code quality tools and ensure that
255	the repository remains in a high quality state.
256	
257	You will need to install the Git pre-commit hooks once after cloning the
258	repository by running:
259	
260	```sh
261	uv run prek install
262	```
263	
264	This should force `prek` to run before all local commits.
265	
266	You can also manually run `prek` by using:
267	
268	```sh
269	uv run prek
270	```
271	
272	The `prek` configuration is stored in `prek.toml`. It configures many
273	pre-commit checks and runs `ruff`, `basedpyright` and `black`.
274	
275	### ruff
276	
277	[ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) is both a fast linter AND code formatter
278	for Python. This project primarily uses it to lint code.
279	
280	You can run it via:
281	
282	```sh
283	uv run ruff check
284	```
285	
286	This can prevent common Python mistakes.
287	
288	### basedpyright
289	
290	[basedpyright](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/) is a fork of `pyright`.
291	It works as a static type-checker for Python code.
292	
293	You should include type hints on as much code as possible to help it out and
294	manage programmer expectations. You should also avoid using deprecated types
295	along with the `Any` type when possible.
296	
297	You can manually run `basedpyright` using `uv`:
298	
299	```sh
300	uv run basedpyright
301	```
302	
303	Sometimes libraries may not include enough type information for `basedpyright`.
304	In these instances you can create type stubs. This process can be done by hand,
305	or you can try to use `basedpyright` to generate said stubs for you via a
306	command like:
307	
308	```sh
309	uv run basedpyright --createstub library
310	```
311	
312	These stubs would be placed in the `typings/` directory at the root of the
313	project.
314	
315	Sometimes type stubs may not be enough, in this case you can add [ignore
316	comments](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/configuration/comments/) to the
317	code.
318	
319	```py
320	# pyright: ignore
321	```
322	
323	You can also edit the `pyrightconfig.json` and add specific libraries to
324	`allowedUntypedLibraries` to allow a whole library to be untyped.
325	
326	#### boto3
327	
328	For `boto3` type stubs there is a tool called
329	[mypy_boto3_builder](https://youtype.github.io/mypy_boto3_builder/) which is
330	used to generate type stubs. This is due to the fact that the `boto3` library
331	is updated frequently, is large, and doesn't include good type information.
332	
333	These stubs can be generated by using `uvx` and `mypy-boto3-builder`, however a
334	script has been provided to keep these up to date called
335	`regen-boto3-type-hints.sh` that will do this for you in a consistent manner.
336	You should use this script when updating the `boto3` version in
337	`pyproject.toml` (and keep it pinned to a specific version) or when you want to
338	add type hints for more AWS services.
339	
340	The type information for `boto3` is stored in the `vendored/` directory.
341	
342	> [!note]
343	> The way Python `.whl` files work means that regenerating them will always
344	> result in a different file as they contain timestamps within them. And the
345	> way `uv.lock` files work is that they store a hash of the `.whl` file. This
346	> is why the `vendored/` file is stored in the repository.
347	
348	When you refer to these types, you should use `TYPE_CHECKING` to guard their
349	inclusion in your code and replace them with stub definitions. For example:
350	
351	```py
352	from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any
353	
354	if TYPE_CHECKING:
355	    from types_boto3_dynamodb.client import DynamoDBClient
356	    from types_boto3_dynamodb.type_defs import (
357	        AttributeValueTypeDef,
358	        UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef,
359	    )
360	else:
361	    DynamoDBClient = Any
362	    AttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
363	    UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
364	```
365	
366	This is because the types are not included when the Python code is packaged and
367	built for AWS SAM deployments.
368	
369	### black
370	
371	[black](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) is a code formatter for
372	Python.
373	
374	You can run `black` directly on a specific file using:
375	
376	```sh
377	uv run black path/to/file.py
378	```
379	
380	`black` is used to ensure all Python code follows the same standard of
381	formatting.
382

# GDPR Right to Erasure Orchestration Platform

[![made-with-python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Made%20with-Python-1f425f.svg)](https://www.python.org/)
[![prek](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/j178/prek/master/docs/assets/badge-v0.json)](https://github.com/j178/prek)
[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)
[![pipeline status](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/pipeline.svg)](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/commits/master)
![coverage](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/coverage.svg?job=test)

This project was originally created to replace the
[Tines](https://www.tines.com/) based Right to Erasure Orchestrator.

## Project Goals

The primary goal of this project is to replace the existing Tines based
orchestrator for subject erasure requests in regards to the GDPR legislation.

The main problems being solved are as followed:

- The costs related to the use of Tines
- The use of Jira as a point of integration and state storage

This project also moves the ownership of the orchestration into the Data
Engineering team.

## API

The orchestrator exposes a private REST API (deployed via AWS API Gateway and
restricted to the configured VPC endpoint). All requests must include the
`x-api-key` header.

### `POST /erasure-request`

Creates a new erasure request and queues it for processing.

```sh
curl -k -sS -X POST \
  'https://omqlg03jma.execute-api.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/prod/erasure-request' \
  -H 'x-api-key: <api-key>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "id": "<unique-id>",
    "source": "onetrust",
    "date_issued": "2026-01-01T12:00:00+00:00",
    "erasure_details": { "email": "user@example.com" }
  }'
```

Required fields: `id`, `source`, `date_issued` (ISO-8601), `erasure_details`
(object with keys from `email`, `first-name`, `last-name`, `postcode`,
`phone-number`, `rfl`, `last-order`). Optional: `site_ids` (list of integers).

| Status | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| `200` | Request accepted; body is `{"id": "<id>"}` |
| `400` | Invalid or malformed request body |
| `409` | A request with this `id` already exists |
| `500` | Internal error |

### `GET /status/{id}`

Returns the audit log entries for an erasure request, ordered chronologically
(oldest first). Backed by `reporter_lambda`, which reads from DynamoDB and
falls back to the materialised S3 audit log.

```sh
curl -k -sS -G \
  'https://omqlg03jma.execute-api.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/prod/status/<id>' \
  -H 'x-api-key: <api-key>'
```

A `200` response has the form:

```json
{
  "id": "<id>",
  "entries": [
    { "id": "<id>", "timestamp": "<iso-8601>", "message": "<text>" }
  ]
}
```

The endpoint returns `404` if no audit entries exist for the given id, and
`400` if the id path parameter is missing or blank.

## Audit log lifecycle

Audit log entries are written to DynamoDB throughout request processing, keyed
by `(erasure_id, timestamp_ms)`.

Once a request is successfully processed and reaches the terminal `done` state,
the process Lambda archives the complete audit log to S3 as a single gzipped
JSON Lines object at `s3://{AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET}/{AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX}/{id}.jsonl.gz`,
then deletes the DynamoDB entries for that id.

The `GET /status/{id}` endpoint reads from DynamoDB first and falls back to the
S3 archive automatically, so reads are seamless before and after archival.

### Environment variables (process Lambda)

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` | Request storage table |
| `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` | GSI name for state-based queries |
| `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE` | Audit log DynamoDB table |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET` | S3 bucket for materialised audit log archives |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX` | Key prefix for archive objects within the bucket |

All five are wired automatically in SAM deployments via `template.yaml`
parameters. When running locally, set them in a `.env` file at the project
root.

## Project Details

This project is built using Python 3.13+ and uses
[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) as a package manager and build tool.

> [!note]
> If you need help setting up your development environment, please refer to
> [docs/setup-local-dev.md](/docs/setup-local-dev.md).

## Development

After cloning this repository you should run:

```sh
uv sync --all-groups
uv run prek install
```

This should create a virtual environment under `.venv` in the local directory,
download all dependencies, and install a Git pre-commit hook.

Generally speaking, if you want to run a normal Python command you should
prefix it with `uv run`.

### Testing

This project uses `pytest` for unit testing. These should be run using the
following command:

```sh
uv run pytest
```

`pytest-cov` is used as an extension that can help you see how much coverage
the tests are actually giving. You can run this as follows:

```sh
uv run pytest --cov=dns_de_rte_orchestrator
```

There isn't a set test coverage percentage code should have, but key files
should generally have more coverage than others.

### Structure

The key entry points for AWS Lambda are:

- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py`

The storage logic sits primarily in the `RequestRepository` parent class and its
implementations in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py`.

The business logic sits in the `RequestProcessor` class in
`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`.

### Managing Dependencies

`pip` commands should not be used to manage dependencies, instead you should
use commands such as:

```sh
# To add a library dependency
uv add library
# To add a development dependency only
uv add --dev pytest
# To remove a dependency
uv remove library
```

This should update the `uv.lock` file, but if in doubt you can run `uv lock`
to regenerate it.

You can attempt to upgrade packages using the following `uv` command:

```sh
uv lock --upgrade-package library
```

## Deployment

The code in this project is deployed as 2 separate AWS Lambdas using [AWS
SAM](https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/).

The versions of Python used during development, testing, and deployment should
all match as closely as possible.

GitLab runners are used to execute tests and deployments. The configuration for
this is managed in `.gitlab-ci.yml`.

### SAM

AWS SAM uses a custom `Makefile` for building the package that gets uploaded to
AWS. This `Makefile` should not be invoked manually. The gist of it is:

- It generates a `requirements.txt` with all the non-dev dependencies based on
  the `uv.lock` file
- It installs all these requirements into the artifact directory
- It copies the project `src/` code into the artifact directory
- It deletes the `requirements.txt` file

The reason for the custom process is to reduce the size of the uploaded
artifact and to keep it replicable across different AWS SAM versions.

> [!note]
> `sam local invoke` has problems when combined with the
> [ServerlessAccessManager](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/platform-as-a-service/serverless-applications/serverless-access-manager/)
> that the DSO team provide for us to use. You'll need to comment it out and
> any lines that reference it in order to get `sam local invoke` to work.

To do a manual deployment you'll need to do the following:

```sh
# First run the build process, this will execute the Makefile
sam build
# Next deploy
sam deploy \
  --config-file ../../samconfig.toml \
  --config-env dev \
  --template-file .aws-sam/build/template.yaml
```

Note that you'll need to point at the `samconfig.toml` in the project root. By
default AWS SAM will try and search the same directory as the template file for
the `samconfig.toml`.

## Code Quality Tools

This project makes use of several tools to ensure consistent code quality.

You can run these tools locally alongside `pytest` by running:

```sh
./local-ci.sh
```

### prek

[prek](https://prek.j178.dev/) is a replacement for the
[pre-commit](https://pre-commit.com/) framework. Both allow you to run scripts
and checks before a Git commit is created.

`prek` is primarily used to run the other code quality tools and ensure that
the repository remains in a high quality state.

You will need to install the Git pre-commit hooks once after cloning the
repository by running:

```sh
uv run prek install
```

This should force `prek` to run before all local commits.

You can also manually run `prek` by using:

```sh
uv run prek
```

The `prek` configuration is stored in `prek.toml`. It configures many
pre-commit checks and runs `ruff`, `basedpyright` and `black`.

### ruff

[ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) is both a fast linter AND code formatter
for Python. This project primarily uses it to lint code.

You can run it via:

```sh
uv run ruff check
```

This can prevent common Python mistakes.

### basedpyright

[basedpyright](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/) is a fork of `pyright`.
It works as a static type-checker for Python code.

You should include type hints on as much code as possible to help it out and
manage programmer expectations. You should also avoid using deprecated types
along with the `Any` type when possible.

You can manually run `basedpyright` using `uv`:

```sh
uv run basedpyright
```

Sometimes libraries may not include enough type information for `basedpyright`.
In these instances you can create type stubs. This process can be done by hand,
or you can try to use `basedpyright` to generate said stubs for you via a
command like:

```sh
uv run basedpyright --createstub library
```

These stubs would be placed in the `typings/` directory at the root of the
project.

Sometimes type stubs may not be enough, in this case you can add [ignore
comments](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/configuration/comments/) to the
code.

```py
# pyright: ignore
```

You can also edit the `pyrightconfig.json` and add specific libraries to
`allowedUntypedLibraries` to allow a whole library to be untyped.

#### boto3

For `boto3` type stubs there is a tool called
[mypy_boto3_builder](https://youtype.github.io/mypy_boto3_builder/) which is
used to generate type stubs. This is due to the fact that the `boto3` library
is updated frequently, is large, and doesn't include good type information.

These stubs can be generated by using `uvx` and `mypy-boto3-builder`, however a
script has been provided to keep these up to date called
`regen-boto3-type-hints.sh` that will do this for you in a consistent manner.
You should use this script when updating the `boto3` version in
`pyproject.toml` (and keep it pinned to a specific version) or when you want to
add type hints for more AWS services.

The type information for `boto3` is stored in the `vendored/` directory.

> [!note]
> The way Python `.whl` files work means that regenerating them will always
> result in a different file as they contain timestamps within them. And the
> way `uv.lock` files work is that they store a hash of the `.whl` file. This
> is why the `vendored/` file is stored in the repository.

When you refer to these types, you should use `TYPE_CHECKING` to guard their
inclusion in your code and replace them with stub definitions. For example:

```py
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from types_boto3_dynamodb.client import DynamoDBClient
    from types_boto3_dynamodb.type_defs import (
        AttributeValueTypeDef,
        UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef,
    )
else:
    DynamoDBClient = Any
    AttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
    UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
```

This is because the types are not included when the Python code is packaged and
built for AWS SAM deployments.

### black

[black](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) is a code formatter for
Python.

You can run `black` directly on a specific file using:

```sh
uv run black path/to/file.py
```

`black` is used to ensure all Python code follows the same standard of
formatting.

# Plan: Address MR !62 review comment #6 — move env-vars section in README

## Context

LyndonHB left an unresolved comment on `README.md:111` (the blank line immediately after the "Environment variables (process Lambda)" table):

> "Might want to move this around in the README."

The current README structure has a subsection `### Environment variables (process Lambda)` (lines 98–110) sitting inside `## Audit log lifecycle` (lines 85–110). The table lists all five process Lambda env vars:

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` | Request storage table |
| `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` | GSI name for state-based queries |
| `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE` | Audit log DynamoDB table |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET` | S3 bucket for materialised audit log archives |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX` | Key prefix for archive objects within the bucket |

Only the bottom three of those five are audit-specific. Having `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` and `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` inside the audit lifecycle section is misleading. More importantly, environment variables are a **deployment / configuration** concern, not part of the lifecycle prose.

The intended outcome: lift the env-vars subsection out of `## Audit log lifecycle` and place it under `## Deployment` where it belongs — adjacent to the SAM build/deploy instructions.

## Approach

### Changes in `README.md`

1. **Remove** the `### Environment variables (process Lambda)` heading, the table, and the two-sentence paragraph that follows it (lines 98–111 of the current file) from inside `## Audit log lifecycle`.

2. **Update the tail** of `## Audit log lifecycle` to end cleanly after the archival description (the sentence currently ending at line 97: "…reads are seamless before and after archival."). The section keeps its two paragraphs and nothing else.

3. **Insert** the lifted content under `## Deployment` as a new sub-section `### Process Lambda environment variables`, placed immediately **before** the existing `### SAM` subsection (currently line 202). The inserted block:

```markdown
### Process Lambda environment variables

All five are wired automatically in SAM deployments via `template.yaml`
parameters. When running locally, set them in a `.env` file at the project
root.

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` | Request storage table |
| `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` | GSI name for state-based queries |
| `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE` | Audit log DynamoDB table |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET` | S3 bucket for materialised audit log archives |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX` | Key prefix for archive objects within the bucket |
```

(The introductory sentence "All five are wired automatically…" moves with the table — it no longer makes sense in the audit section without the table.)

### Files modified

- `README.md` — sole change; move and re-home one subsection.

### Files NOT modified

- No code files, no tests, no SAM template.

## Verification

1. `grep -n "Environment variables" README.md` — confirms the heading now appears under the Deployment section (after line ~195) and is absent from the Audit log lifecycle section.
2. Read `## Audit log lifecycle` — confirms it ends with the archival/fallback description and has no env-var table.
3. Read `## Deployment` — confirms the new `### Process Lambda environment variables` subsection appears before `### SAM`.
4. Visually scan the overall README flow: API → Audit log lifecycle → Project Details → Development → Deployment (with env vars) → SAM → Code Quality Tools.

## Out of scope

All 5 code review comments (#1–#5) are already implemented and committed. This is the final outstanding comment.

# Plan: Address MR !62 review comment #5 — typed `DuplicateRequestError` from `RequestRepository`

## Context

LyndonHB left an unresolved comment on `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py:157`:

> "We should take a note and make `request_repo` throw a more sensible exception so it is easier to know what has happened (i.e. a duplicate key was raised)"

Today the `creator_lambda` discriminates duplicate-id failures by sniffing the AWS `ClientError`'s error code:

```python
except ClientError as e:
    if e.response.get("Error", {}).get("Code") == "ConditionalCheckFailedException":
        return _response(409, ...)
```

This leaks DynamoDB-specific knowledge into the lambda. The two issues:

1. **Layer leak** — `creator_lambda` is the HTTP boundary; it shouldn't know what storage backend the repo uses or what error code AWS returns. If a future implementer swaps the backend (or adds an in-memory mode for local dev), the duplicate path stops returning 409 — the in-memory repo today raises a plain `ValueError` (`request_repository.py:91`) which today matches *no* branch and falls into the generic 500.
2. **Asymmetry between repo implementations** — `InMemoryRequestRepository.create` raises `ValueError(f"{request.id} already exists")` while `DynamoDBRequestRepository.create` raises `botocore.exceptions.ClientError`. There is no shared exception type the lambda can catch.

The intended outcome: introduce a domain-level `DuplicateRequestError` in `request_repository.py`, raise it from both `InMemoryRequestRepository.create` and `DynamoDBRequestRepository.create` when a duplicate id is detected, and have `creator_lambda` catch it directly to return 409. The boundary lambda no longer touches DynamoDB error codes; both repos behave symmetrically.

## Approach

### Changes in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py`

1. Add a new exception class at the module level (near the `RequestRepository` ABC, before `InMemoryRequestRepository`):
   ```python
   class DuplicateRequestError(Exception):
       """Raised by RequestRepository.create when a request with the same id already exists."""

       def __init__(self, id: str) -> None:
           self.id: str = id
           super().__init__(f"Erasure request with id '{id}' already exists")
   ```
   Plain `Exception` subclass (not `ValueError`) so callers can catch it without losing the existing `ValueError` semantics elsewhere.

2. Update `InMemoryRequestRepository.create` (`request_repository.py:89–92`) to raise the new error:
   ```python
   if request.id in self._db:
       raise DuplicateRequestError(request.id)
   ```

3. Update `DynamoDBRequestRepository.create` (`request_repository.py:144–153`) to translate the boto `ConditionalCheckFailedException` into the new error. The cleanest place is inside `create` itself — wrap the `put_item` call:
   ```python
   try:
       _response = self.dynamodb.put_item(
           TableName=self.table_name,
           Item=item,
           ReturnValues="NONE",
           ConditionExpression=self.NOT_EXISTS_CONDITION,
       )
   except ClientError as e:
       if e.response.get("Error", {}).get("Code") == "ConditionalCheckFailedException":
           raise DuplicateRequestError(request.id) from e
       raise
   ```
   Add the `from botocore.exceptions import ClientError` import at the top if not already present (it isn't today — `creator_lambda` imports it directly).

4. Leave `update`'s `ValueError` ("does not already exists") alone — that path is not part of this comment and only used by the in-memory repo.

### Changes in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py`

5. Replace the `ClientError` sniff (lines 150–163) with a `DuplicateRequestError` catch. The boundary now reads cleanly without knowing about DynamoDB:
   ```python
   try:
       if in_dry_run_mode:
           logger.warning("DryRun: Would have created request for %s", request.id)
       else:
           request_repo.create(request)
   except DuplicateRequestError:
       logger.warning("Duplicate erasure request rejected: %s", request.id)
       return _response(409, {"error": "conflict", "id": request.id})
   except Exception:
       logger.exception("Failed to create erasure request %s", str(request))
       return _response(500, {"error": "internal error"})
   ```
   Drop the now-unused `from botocore.exceptions import ClientError` import.

   Add the new import: `from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_repository import DuplicateRequestError` (alongside the existing `RequestRepository, get_request_repository` import on line 20 — extend that one).

### Test updates

6. **`tests/test_request_repository.py`** — the existing test at line 100 asserts `ValueError, match="req-123 already exists"` from the in-memory repo. Update it to `DuplicateRequestError` and assert the `id` attribute is set. Add a parallel test for `DynamoDBRequestRepository.create` translating `ConditionalCheckFailedException` into `DuplicateRequestError` (using a `MagicMock` dynamodb client whose `put_item` raises `ClientError`).

7. **`tests/test_creator_lambda.py`** — the test at line 197 (`test_lambda_handler_returns_409_on_duplicate_id`) currently makes the mock repo raise a `ClientError`. Change it to raise `DuplicateRequestError("req-123")` instead. The 500-on-other-`ClientError` test (line 209) is no longer load-bearing for the duplicate path but is still valid (a generic ClientError now falls into the bare `except Exception` and returns 500) — verify the assertion still holds; if so, leave it alone.

### Files modified

- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py` — new `DuplicateRequestError`, raise it from both `create` implementations.
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py` — catch `DuplicateRequestError` instead of sniffing `ClientError`.
- `tests/test_request_repository.py` — update the in-memory duplicate test, add a Dynamo duplicate test.
- `tests/test_creator_lambda.py` — update the 409 test to use `DuplicateRequestError`.

### Files NOT modified

- `RequestRepository.update`'s `ValueError` — not part of this comment.
- Any other call site of `request_repo.create` — `creator_lambda` is the only caller.

### Open TODO (`TODO.md`)

The reviewer's comment was prefaced "we should take a note and …", implying this was tracked as future work. The branch's `TODO.md` already has a `need-more-detail` open item — once this is implemented and merged, no TODO entry is needed. (No new TODO line should be added.)

## Verification

1. `uv run pytest tests/test_request_repository.py tests/test_creator_lambda.py -q` — both updated tests plus the new Dynamo translation test pass.
2. `uv run pytest -q` — full suite green.
3. `uv run basedpyright src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py` — no new diagnostics.
4. Visual sanity: `creator_lambda` no longer imports `botocore.exceptions.ClientError`; both repo implementations raise the same `DuplicateRequestError` for the duplicate-id case.

## Out of scope

The remaining 1 unresolved review comment (#6, README reorder) is tracked separately. Comments #1–#4 are already implemented and committed.

# Plan: Address MR !62 review comment #6 — move env-vars section in README

## Context

LyndonHB left an unresolved comment on `README.md:111` (the blank line immediately after the "Environment variables (process Lambda)" table):

> "Might want to move this around in the README."

The current README structure has a subsection `### Environment variables (process Lambda)` (lines 98–110) sitting inside `## Audit log lifecycle` (lines 85–110). The table lists all five process Lambda env vars:

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` | Request storage table |
| `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` | GSI name for state-based queries |
| `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE` | Audit log DynamoDB table |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET` | S3 bucket for materialised audit log archives |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX` | Key prefix for archive objects within the bucket |

Only the bottom three of those five are audit-specific. Having `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` and `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` inside the audit lifecycle section is misleading. More importantly, environment variables are a **deployment / configuration** concern, not part of the lifecycle prose.

The intended outcome: lift the env-vars subsection out of `## Audit log lifecycle` and place it under `## Deployment` where it belongs — adjacent to the SAM build/deploy instructions.

## Approach

### Changes in `README.md`

1. **Remove** the `### Environment variables (process Lambda)` heading, the table, and the two-sentence paragraph that follows it (lines 98–111 of the current file) from inside `## Audit log lifecycle`.

2. **Update the tail** of `## Audit log lifecycle` to end cleanly after the archival description (the sentence currently ending at line 97: "…reads are seamless before and after archival."). The section keeps its two paragraphs and nothing else.

3. **Insert** the lifted content under `## Deployment` as a new sub-section `### Process Lambda environment variables`, placed immediately **before** the existing `### SAM` subsection (currently line 202). The inserted block:

```markdown
### Process Lambda environment variables

All five are wired automatically in SAM deployments via `template.yaml`
parameters. When running locally, set them in a `.env` file at the project
root.

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` | Request storage table |
| `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` | GSI name for state-based queries |
| `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE` | Audit log DynamoDB table |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET` | S3 bucket for materialised audit log archives |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX` | Key prefix for archive objects within the bucket |
```

(The introductory sentence "All five are wired automatically…" moves with the table — it no longer makes sense in the audit section without the table.)

### Files modified

- `README.md` — sole change; move and re-home one subsection.

### Files NOT modified

- No code files, no tests, no SAM template.

## Verification

1. `grep -n "Environment variables" README.md` — confirms the heading now appears under the Deployment section (after line ~195) and is absent from the Audit log lifecycle section.
2. Read `## Audit log lifecycle` — confirms it ends with the archival/fallback description and has no env-var table.
3. Read `## Deployment` — confirms the new `### Process Lambda environment variables` subsection appears before `### SAM`.
4. Visually scan the overall README flow: API → Audit log lifecycle → Project Details → Development → Deployment (with env vars) → SAM → Code Quality Tools.

## Out of scope

All 5 code review comments (#1–#5) are already implemented and committed. This is the final outstanding comment.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/saurabhtyagi/.claude/skills/commit-message

## Context

Data Engineering changes typically touch ingestion pipelines, Airflow DAGs, AWS infrastructure, and source integrations. Commit messages should describe the user-visible intent and impact, not just restate low-level diff details.

## Instructions

1. Run `git diff origin/main..HEAD` to get the diff of the local branch against its remote base.
2. Derive the Jira key from the branch name when it matches `<JIRA-KEY>_<short-name>`.
   If the branch name does not include a Jira key, ask the user for one before generating the final message.
3. Write a one-line commit message in this format:

   ```text
   <JIRA-KEY>: <Imperative subject line>
   ```

4. Follow these rules:
   - Start the subject line with a capital letter.
   - Use imperative mood (command wording), not past tense or present participle.
   - Do not add a trailing period.
   - Do not add signatures or extra metadata.
   - Summarize intent and impact; do not just restate diff details.

## Examples

### Good

- `DA-1234: Fix use of deprecated method`
- `DA-5634: Add RtE cleaner component`

### Bad

- `DA-5634: Adding RtE cleaner component`  
  Reason: uses present participle instead of imperative mood.
- `Edit Common.scala to use method2 instead of method1 - DA-1234`  
  Reason: missing Jira key prefix format.
- `DA-1234: fix use of deprecated method`  
  Reason: subject line should start with a capital letter.
- `DA-1234: Fixed use of deprecated method`  
  Reason: uses past tense instead of imperative mood.
- `DA-5634: RtE cleaner component`  
  Reason: noun phrase instead of action.
- `DA-4634: Increase CPU request from 2 to 3`  
  Reason: too low-level and diff-like; prefer higher-level intent.
- `DA-6093: Add api-ingest tests to the CI pipeline.`  
  Reason: trailing period is not allowed.

Few sentences explaining what changes have been made. Highlight key files changed. Keep this concise and prioritise comments for reviewer benefit.

Brief context of why this change is needed — the motivation, ticket, or problem being solved.

Do not append any additional signature to the MR description.

input.command:
glab mr update 62 --description "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What

Implements audit log archival to S3 when an erasure request is successfully processed. Once a request reaches the \`done\` state, the process Lambda materialises all DynamoDB audit log entries for that id to S3 as a gzipped JSON Lines object (\`{prefix}/{id}.jsonl.gz\`), then deletes the rows from DynamoDB. The existing \`FallbackAuditLogReader\` (used by the reporter Lambda) seamlessly serves reads from S3 after archival with no changes on the read path.

Also addresses all reviewer comments from the first review round: retry-logic isolation, archival call placement, entry sort ordering, a domain-level duplicate-id exception, and README structure.

Key files: \`audit_logging.py\`, \`request_processor.py\`, \`request_repository.py\`, \`creator_lambda.py\`, \`processor_lambda.py\`, \`template.yaml\`, \`README.md\`.

## Why

DATA-4715 — audit log entries were accumulating in DynamoDB indefinitely with no archival path. The S3 fallback reader already existed but had no writer. This ticket closes that gap and reduces long-term DynamoDB table growth.

## Changes

**Audit log archival (core feature)**
- Added \`S3AuditLogWriter\` — symmetric counterpart to the existing \`S3AuditLogReader\`, writes gzipped JSONL to the same key format (\`{prefix}/{id}.jsonl.gz\`); entries are sorted by timestamp ascending before serialisation so the on-disk format is always chronologically ordered regardless of input order
- Added \`DynamoDBAuditLogger.delete_logs()\` — batch-deletes all entries for an id using \`BatchWriteItem\` in chunks of 25; retry logic extracted into \`_batch_write_with_retry\` with named \`BACKOFF_BASE/MAX_SECONDS\` constants and a \`_backoff_seconds(attempt)\` helper (replaces inline bitshift backoff)
- Added \`AuditArchiver\` — orchestrates S3-write-then-DynamoDB-delete with safe ordering: if S3 write fails, DynamoDB rows are preserved
- Added \`get_audit_log_archiver()\` factory wired from env vars
- \`RequestProcessor\` accepts an optional \`AuditArchiver\`; archival is triggered from the \`process()\` success path (not inside \`process_request\`) after a request transitions to \`done\` and not in dry-run mode; archival failures are caught and logged without re-raising
- \`template.yaml\`: added \`ProcessS3WritePolicy\` (\`s3:PutObject\` on the audit bucket/prefix) and \`AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET\`/\`AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX\` env vars to \`ProcessFunction\`

**Bug fixes (surfaced during review)**
- \`request_repository.py\`: introduced \`DuplicateRequestError\` — both \`InMemoryRequestRepository\` and \`DynamoDBRequestRepository\` now raise this domain exception on duplicate ids, translating \`ConditionalCheckFailedException\` at the repo boundary; \`creator_lambda\` catches \`DuplicateRequestError\` directly and no longer inspects AWS error codes
- \`request_processor.py\`: fixed double-assignment bug in \`_transition_request\` (\`message = message = …\` → \`message = message + …\`)

**Tests (new file + extensions)**
- New \`tests/test_s3_audit_log_writer.py\` — moto-backed round-trip tests confirming writer and reader are format-compatible
- Extended \`tests/test_audit_logging.py\` — unit tests for \`S3AuditLogWriter\` (including sort-on-write), \`AuditArchiver\` ordering, \`delete_logs\` chunking and \`UnprocessedItems\` retry, and \`_batch_write_with_retry\` backoff
- Extended \`tests/test_dynamodb_audit_logger.py\` — moto integration tests for \`delete_logs\`
- Extended \`tests/test_request_processor.py\` — archiver triggered on \`done\` via \`process()\`, not triggered in dry-run, exception does not propagate; integration test wiring archival through full \`process()\` sweep
- Extended \`tests/test_creator_lambda.py\` — 409 on duplicate id (via \`DuplicateRequestError\`), 500 on other errors
- Extended \`tests/test_request_repository.py\` — \`DuplicateRequestError\` from in-memory and DynamoDB repos

**Housekeeping**
- \`README.md\`: documented \`POST /erasure-request\` (including 409), audit log lifecycle, and moved process Lambda env vars table to the Deployment section where it belongs
- \`TODO.md\`: ticked off audit log S3 materialisation; added \`need-more-detail\` handling as an open item
EOF
)" 2>&1

1	import json
2	
3	from aws_lambda_powertools import Logger
4	from aws_lambda_powertools.tracing import Tracer
5	from aws_lambda_powertools.utilities.typing import LambdaContext
6	from dotenv import load_dotenv
7	
8	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging import (
9	    FallbackAuditLogReader,
10	    get_audit_log_reader,
11	)
12	
13	# Load .env file, mostly useful for testing
14	_ = load_dotenv()
15	
16	logger = Logger()
17	tracer = Tracer()  # Sets service via POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME env var
18	
19	reader: FallbackAuditLogReader = get_audit_log_reader()
20	
21	
22	def _response(
23	    status_code: int,
24	    body: dict[str, object],
25	) -> dict[str, object]:
26	    return {
27	        "statusCode": status_code,
28	        "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
29	        "body": json.dumps(body),
30	    }
31	
32	
33	@tracer.capture_lambda_handler
34	@logger.inject_lambda_context
35	def lambda_handler(
36	    event: dict[str, object],
37	    _context: LambdaContext,
38	) -> dict[str, object]:
39	    """Entrypoint for AWS Lambda that retrieves the audit log for an erasure id."""
40	    raw_params: object = event.get("pathParameters")
41	    path_parameters: dict[str, object] = (  # pyright: ignore[reportUnknownVariableType]
42	        raw_params if isinstance(raw_params, dict) else {}
43	    )
44	    erasure_id: object = path_parameters.get("id")
45	
46	    if not isinstance(erasure_id, str) or not erasure_id.strip():
47	        return _response(400, {"error": "missing erasure id"})
48	
49	    try:
50	        entries = reader.get_logs(erasure_id)
51	    except Exception:
52	        logger.exception("Failed to retrieve audit log for %s", erasure_id)
53	        return _response(500, {"error": "internal error"})
54	
55	    if not entries:
56	        return _response(404, {"error": "no audit log found", "id": erasure_id})
57	
58	    return _response(
59	        200,
60	        {
61	            "id": erasure_id,
62	            "entries": [entry.to_json() for entry in entries],
63	        },
64	    )

2	Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
3	Description: >
4	  Right to Erasure Orchestrator.
5	  Originally a replacement for the Tines Orchestrator, this stack primarily
6	  consists of 2 AWS Lambdas that communicate with DynamoDB
7	
8	Parameters:
9	  Environment:

25	class DuplicateRequestError(Exception):
26	    """Raised by RequestRepository.create when a request with the same id already exists."""
27	
28	    def __init__(self, id: str) -> None:
29	        self.id: str = id
30	        super().__init__(f"Erasure request with id '{id}' already exists")

33	class RequestRepository(ABC):
34	    """Common interface for dealing with Erasure Request storage"""
35	
36	    @abstractmethod
37	    def create(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
38	        """Create a new erasure request in the underlying storage"""
39	        raise NotImplementedError()
40	
41	    @abstractmethod
42	    def update(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
43	        """
44	        Updates an existing item in the underlying storage based on the given request.
45	
46	        Note that the requests id is used to do the updating.
47	        """
48	        raise NotImplementedError()
49	
50	    @abstractmethod
51	    def get(self, id: str) -> ErasureRequest | None:
52	        """Get a specific request from the underlying storage based upon its id."""
53	        raise NotImplementedError()
54	
55	    @abstractmethod
56	    def get_all_in_state(
57	        self,
58	        state: ErasureState,
59	        limit: int = 100,
60	        from_date: datetime | None = None,
61	    ) -> list[ErasureRequest]:
62	        """
63	        Get the erasure requests within a given state up to a certain limit.
64	
65	        When the limit is reached, that many items are returned. There could be
66	        more items with the given state.
67	
68	        You can optionally set a from_date to filter only results from that
69	        given time and date until now.
70	
71	        Warning: This can return a lot of results if you use a terminal state.
72	
73	        Warning: Implementations may cap the amount of items returned under the
74	        given limit if certain conditions are met. For example, DynamoDB will
75	        stop returning items for a single query if the total size reaches 1 MB.
76	        It is up to implementations as to how much they respect this limit.
77	        For example, a DynamoDB implementation could paginate.
78	
79	        """
80	        raise NotImplementedError()

83	class InMemoryRequestRepository(RequestRepository):
84	    """
85	    An example implementation of a request repository.
86	
87	    This implementation simply stores objects in an internal dictionary.
88	    It does not clone objects.
89	
90	    This implementation is not thread safe.
91	    """
92	
93	    def __init__(self) -> None:
94	        super().__init__()
95	        self._db: dict[str, ErasureRequest] = {}
96	
97	    @override
98	    def create(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
99	        if request.id in self._db:
100	            raise DuplicateRequestError(request.id)
101	        self._db[request.id] = request
102	
103	    @override
104	    def update(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
105	        if request.id not in self._db:
106	            raise ValueError(f"{request.id} does not already exists")
107	        self._db[request.id] = request
108	
109	    @override
110	    def get(self, id: str) -> ErasureRequest | None:
111	        return self._db.get(id)
112	
113	    @override
114	    def get_all_in_state(
115	        self, state: ErasureState, limit: int = 100, from_date: datetime | None = None
116	    ) -> list[ErasureRequest]:
117	        if limit <= 0:
118	            return []
119	        found: list[ErasureRequest] = []
120	        count: int = 0
121	        for _, item in self._db.items():
122	            if item.state == state:
123	                if from_date is not None and item.date_issued < from_date:
124	                    continue
125	                found.append(item)
126	                count += 1
127	                if count >= limit:
128	                    break
129	        return found
130

132	class DynamoDBRequestRepository(RequestRepository):
133	    """DynamoDB backed repository implementation"""
134	
135	    ID_FIELD: Literal["id"] = "id"
136	    STATE_FIELD: Literal["state"] = "state"
137	    DATE_ISSUED_FIELD: Literal["date_issued"] = "date_issued"
138	    SOURCE_FIELD: Literal["source"] = "source"
139	    ERASURE_DETAILS_FIELD: Literal["erasure_details"] = "erasure_details"
140	    SITE_IDS_FIELD: Literal["site_ids"] = "site_ids"
141	    ERASURE_SERVICE_ID_FIELD: Literal["erasure_service_id"] = "erasure_service_id"
142	
143	    NOT_EXISTS_CONDITION: str = f"attribute_not_exists({ID_FIELD})"
144	    EXISTS_CONDITION: str = f"attribute_exists({ID_FIELD})"
145	
146	    def __init__(
147	        self, table_name: str, index_name: str, dynamodb: DynamoDBClient
148	    ) -> None:
149	        super().__init__()
150	        self.table_name: str = table_name
151	        self.index_name: str = index_name
152	        self.dynamodb: DynamoDBClient = dynamodb
153	
154	    @override
155	    def create(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
156	        item = self.item_from_request(request)
157	        try:
158	            # This should error if there is an item in the table with the given id
159	            _response = self.dynamodb.put_item(
160	                TableName=self.table_name,
161	                Item=item,
162	                ReturnValues="NONE",
163	                ConditionExpression=self.NOT_EXISTS_CONDITION,
164	            )
165	        except ClientError as e:
166	            if (
167	                e.response.get("Error", {}).get("Code")
168	                == "ConditionalCheckFailedException"
169	            ):
170	                raise DuplicateRequestError(request.id) from e
171	            raise
172	
173	    @override
174	    def update(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
175	        item = self.item_from_request(request)
176	        # This should error if there is NOT an item in the table with the given
177	        # id
178	        _response = self.dynamodb.put_item(
179	            TableName=self.table_name,
180	            Item=item,
181	            ReturnValues="NONE",
182	            ConditionExpression=self.EXISTS_CONDITION,
183	        )
184	
185	    @override
186	    def get(self, id: str) -> ErasureRequest | None:
187	        response = self.dynamodb.get_item(
188	            TableName=self.table_name, Key={self.ID_FIELD: {"S": id}}
189	        )
190	
191	        if "Item" in response:
192	            item: dict[str, AttributeValueTypeDef] = response["Item"]
193	            # Convert the item back to a domain object
194	            return self.response_to_request(item)
195	        else:
196	            return None
197	
198	    @override
199	    def get_all_in_state(
200	        self,
201	        state: ErasureState,
202	        limit: int = 100,
203	        from_date: datetime | None = None,
204	    ) -> list[ErasureRequest]:
205	        if limit <= 0:
206	            return []
207	        key_condition: str = "#S = :state"
208	
209	        #  The attribute values being used in the query
210	        attribute_values: Mapping[str, UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef] = {
211	            ":state": {"S": state}
212	        }
213	        # Replacements for attribute names that are reserved keywords
214	        attribute_names: Mapping[str, str] = {"#S": "state"}
215	
216	        if from_date is not None:
217	            # Add on the sort key condition to the key_condition
218	            key_condition += " AND date_issued >= :date"

input.content:
# Plan: Merge origin/master into DATA_4715_audit_log_to_s3

## Context

`origin/master` has advanced 2 commits since our branch diverged (`a362723`):
- `64aecc7` — "Implement Phase-2 and Phase-3 Customer erasure process"
- `09dd169` — Merge commit

The master changes implement Phase-2 (Customer Erasures API, Zendesk) and Phase-3 by adding `CustomerErasureAPI`, `ZendeskErasureAPI`, `customer_erasures_api_client.py`, `zendesk.py`, wiring them into `RequestProcessor`, `processor_lambda`, and expanding `test_request_processor.py` substantially.

A dry-run `git merge origin/master` produced **4 conflict files**:

1. `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py` — AUTO-MERGED cleanly (no conflict marker left; git resolved it)
2. `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py` — CONFLICT
3. `template.yaml` — CONFLICT
4. `tests/test_request_processor.py` — CONFLICT

## Conflict analysis and resolution approach

### 1. `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`

Two independent conflicts:

**Conflict A — `__init__` signature (lines ~54–66 with markers)**

- `HEAD` (ours): adds `archiver: AuditArchiver | None = None` parameter and `self.archiver` assignment; removes `erasures_api`/`zendesk` params.
- `origin/master` (theirs): adds `erasures_api: CustomerErasureAPI`, `zendesk: ZendeskErasureAPI` params and their assignments; keeps archiver absent.

Resolution — **take both**: the merged `__init__` must include all four new parameters in correct declaration order:
```python
def __init__(
    self,
    repository: RequestRepository,
    audit: AuditLogger,
    as400: AS400Notifier,
    customer_api: CustomerAPIClient,
    erasures_api: CustomerErasureAPI,
    zendesk: ZendeskErasureAPI,
    dry_run: bool = False,
    archiver: AuditArchiver | None = None,
) -> None:
    ...
    self.customer_api: CustomerAPIClient = customer_api
    self.erasures_api: CustomerErasureAPI = erasures_api
    self.zendesk: ZendeskErasureAPI = zendesk
    self.archiver: AuditArchiver | None = archiver
    if dry_run:
        ...
```
Imports must include `CustomerErasureAPI`, `ZendeskErasureAPI` etc. from master's additions, PLUS `AuditArchiver` from ours.

**Conflict B — `process()` try/except block (lines ~148–170 with markers)**

- `HEAD` (ours): replaces `successful_request_count += 1` before the except with a `continue` inside the except, then increments `successful_request_count` and calls `self._archive_if_done(request)` on the success path.
- `origin/master` (theirs): adds a second `except Exception:` broad catch with a descriptive comment and `failed_request_count += 1`.

Resolution — **take both**: keep our success-path structure (`continue` in the `ErasureRequestException` handler, then `successful_request_count += 1; self._archive_if_done(request)` after the try block) AND add master's broad `except Exception` below the `ErasureRequestException` handler:
```python
try:
    self.process_request(request)
except ErasureRequestException:
    logger.exception("Problem processing request %s", request.id)
    failed_request_count += 1
    continue
except Exception:
    # This branch will catch any bubbled up exceptions not wrapped by
    # domain exceptions. This should not really happen as we should be
    # handling the possible error states.
    logger.exception("Unknown problem processing request %s", request.id)
    failed_request_count += 1
    continue
successful_request_count += 1
self._archive_if_done(request)
```

The imports at the top of `request_processor.py` must also include master's additions (`CustomerErasureAPI`, `CustomerErasureAPIException`, `ZendeskErasureAPI`, `ZendeskTicketId`, `ZendeskUserId`).

### 2. `template.yaml`

The conflict region is around the `ProcessFunction` SAM resource. Our branch added `AuditLogS3Bucket`/`AuditLogS3Prefix` parameters and the `ProcessS3WritePolicy` IAM resource. Master added the `CustomerErasuresApiUrl`, `ZendeskApiUrl`, `ZendeskApiToken` parameters and their env vars to `ProcessFunction`.

Resolution — **take both** additively: all parameters from both branches survive; `ProcessFunction.Environment.Variables` includes all env vars from both; `ProcessS3WritePolicy` (ours) and any new IAM resources from master both survive.

### 3. `tests/test_request_processor.py`

Two conflicts at the top:

**Conflict A — imports**
- `HEAD`: `from pytest import MonkeyPatch` + `AuditArchiver, NoOpAuditLogger`
- master: `from _pytest.logging import LogCaptureFixture` + `InMemoryAuditLogger, NoOpAuditLogger` + many new imports (`CustomerErasureAPI`, `ZendeskErasureAPI`, etc.)

Resolution — **take all**: `from pytest import MonkeyPatch` (ours) AND `from _pytest.logging import LogCaptureFixture` (theirs); `AuditArchiver` from ours; `InMemoryAuditLogger` and the new API imports from theirs.

**Conflict B — `ErasureState` import**
- `HEAD`: `from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import ErasureRequest, ErasureState`
- master: `from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import ErasureRequest` (no `ErasureState`)

Resolution — keep `ErasureState` (ours uses it for `_make_request(state: ErasureState)` helper) while also accepting master's new imports. Check if master's test additions use `ErasureState`; if not, keeping it is harmless.

The `_build_processor` factory helper in the test file also needs to be updated to pass the new required `erasures_api` and `zendesk` params when constructing `RequestProcessor` — both branches will have touched this.

### 4. `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`

Git reports auto-merged (no conflict markers). After merge, `processor_lambda.py` will include master's `customer_erasures_api`/`zendesk_api` wiring AND our archiver wiring. The `RequestProcessor(...)` call must pass all arguments: `request_repo, audit, as400, customer_api, customer_erasures_api, zendesk_api, dry_run=in_dry_run_mode, archiver=archiver`.

## Step-by-step implementation

1. `git merge origin/master` — starts the merge, leaves conflict markers.
2. Resolve `request_processor.py` — take both sides of both conflicts as described.
3. Resolve `template.yaml` — take all parameters and env vars from both sides.
4. Resolve `tests/test_request_processor.py` — take all imports from both sides; verify `_build_processor` passes `erasures_api` and `zendesk` to `RequestProcessor`.
5. Check `processor_lambda.py` auto-merge result — verify `RequestProcessor(...)` call includes all 8 arguments.
6. `uv run pytest -q` — full suite must be green.
7. `uv run basedpyright` — no new errors.
8. `git add` resolved files + `git commit` with a merge commit.
9. `git push origin DATA_4715_audit_log_to_s3`.

## Files modified

- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py` — conflict resolution (both sets of changes)
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py` — verify auto-merge; fix `RequestProcessor` call args
- `template.yaml` — conflict resolution (all parameters/env vars)
- `tests/test_request_processor.py` — conflict resolution (all imports; `_build_processor` factory args)

## Verification

1. `uv run pytest -q` — all tests green (expected ~220+ tests from both branches combined)
2. `uv run basedpyright` — 0 errors, 0 warnings
3. `sam validate --lint` — SAM template valid
4. `git log --oneline origin/master..HEAD` — shows our 11 branch commits + the merge commit on top

# Plan: Address MR !62 review comment #6 — move env-vars section in README

## Context

LyndonHB left an unresolved comment on `README.md:111` (the blank line immediately after the "Environment variables (process Lambda)" table):

> "Might want to move this around in the README."

The current README structure has a subsection `### Environment variables (process Lambda)` (lines 98–110) sitting inside `## Audit log lifecycle` (lines 85–110). The table lists all five process Lambda env vars:

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` | Request storage table |
| `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` | GSI name for state-based queries |
| `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE` | Audit log DynamoDB table |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET` | S3 bucket for materialised audit log archives |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX` | Key prefix for archive objects within the bucket |

Only the bottom three of those five are audit-specific. Having `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` and `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` inside the audit lifecycle section is misleading. More importantly, environment variables are a **deployment / configuration** concern, not part of the lifecycle prose.

The intended outcome: lift the env-vars subsection out of `## Audit log lifecycle` and place it under `## Deployment` where it belongs — adjacent to the SAM build/deploy instructions.

## Approach

### Changes in `README.md`

1. **Remove** the `### Environment variables (process Lambda)` heading, the table, and the two-sentence paragraph that follows it (lines 98–111 of the current file) from inside `## Audit log lifecycle`.

2. **Update the tail** of `## Audit log lifecycle` to end cleanly after the archival description (the sentence currently ending at line 97: "…reads are seamless before and after archival."). The section keeps its two paragraphs and nothing else.

3. **Insert** the lifted content under `## Deployment` as a new sub-section `### Process Lambda environment variables`, placed immediately **before** the existing `### SAM` subsection (currently line 202). The inserted block:

```markdown
### Process Lambda environment variables

All five are wired automatically in SAM deployments via `template.yaml`
parameters. When running locally, set them in a `.env` file at the project
root.

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `DYNAMO_DB_TABLE` | Request storage table |
| `DYNAMO_DB_INDEX` | GSI name for state-based queries |
| `DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE` | Audit log DynamoDB table |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET` | S3 bucket for materialised audit log archives |
| `AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX` | Key prefix for archive objects within the bucket |
```

(The introductory sentence "All five are wired automatically…" moves with the table — it no longer makes sense in the audit section without the table.)

### Files modified

- `README.md` — sole change; move and re-home one subsection.

### Files NOT modified

- No code files, no tests, no SAM template.

## Verification

1. `grep -n "Environment variables" README.md` — confirms the heading now appears under the Deployment section (after line ~195) and is absent from the Audit log lifecycle section.
2. Read `## Audit log lifecycle` — confirms it ends with the archival/fallback description and has no env-var table.
3. Read `## Deployment` — confirms the new `### Process Lambda environment variables` subsection appears before `### SAM`.
4. Visually scan the overall README flow: API → Audit log lifecycle → Project Details → Development → Deployment (with env vars) → SAM → Code Quality Tools.

## Out of scope

All 5 code review comments (#1–#5) are already implemented and committed. This is the final outstanding comment.

input.plan:
# Plan: Merge origin/master into DATA_4715_audit_log_to_s3

## Context

`origin/master` has advanced 2 commits since our branch diverged (`a362723`):
- `64aecc7` — "Implement Phase-2 and Phase-3 Customer erasure process"
- `09dd169` — Merge commit

The master changes implement Phase-2 (Customer Erasures API, Zendesk) and Phase-3 by adding `CustomerErasureAPI`, `ZendeskErasureAPI`, `customer_erasures_api_client.py`, `zendesk.py`, wiring them into `RequestProcessor`, `processor_lambda`, and expanding `test_request_processor.py` substantially.

A dry-run `git merge origin/master` produced **4 conflict files**:

1. `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py` — AUTO-MERGED cleanly (no conflict marker left; git resolved it)
2. `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py` — CONFLICT
3. `template.yaml` — CONFLICT
4. `tests/test_request_processor.py` — CONFLICT

## Conflict analysis and resolution approach

### 1. `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`

Two independent conflicts:

**Conflict A — `__init__` signature (lines ~54–66 with markers)**

- `HEAD` (ours): adds `archiver: AuditArchiver | None = None` parameter and `self.archiver` assignment; removes `erasures_api`/`zendesk` params.
- `origin/master` (theirs): adds `erasures_api: CustomerErasureAPI`, `zendesk: ZendeskErasureAPI` params and their assignments; keeps archiver absent.

Resolution — **take both**: the merged `__init__` must include all four new parameters in correct declaration order:
```python
def __init__(
    self,
    repository: RequestRepository,
    audit: AuditLogger,
    as400: AS400Notifier,
    customer_api: CustomerAPIClient,
    erasures_api: CustomerErasureAPI,
    zendesk: ZendeskErasureAPI,
    dry_run: bool = False,
    archiver: AuditArchiver | None = None,
) -> None:
    ...
    self.customer_api: CustomerAPIClient = customer_api
    self.erasures_api: CustomerErasureAPI = erasures_api
    self.zendesk: ZendeskErasureAPI = zendesk
    self.archiver: AuditArchiver | None = archiver
    if dry_run:
        ...
```
Imports must include `CustomerErasureAPI`, `ZendeskErasureAPI` etc. from master's additions, PLUS `AuditArchiver` from ours.

**Conflict B — `process()` try/except block (lines ~148–170 with markers)**

- `HEAD` (ours): replaces `successful_request_count += 1` before the except with a `continue` inside the except, then increments `successful_request_count` and calls `self._archive_if_done(request)` on the success path.
- `origin/master` (theirs): adds a second `except Exception:` broad catch with a descriptive comment and `failed_request_count += 1`.

Resolution — **take both**: keep our success-path structure (`continue` in the `ErasureRequestException` handler, then `successful_request_count += 1; self._archive_if_done(request)` after the try block) AND add master's broad `except Exception` below the `ErasureRequestException` handler:
```python
try:
    self.process_request(request)
except ErasureRequestException:
    logger.exception("Problem processing request %s", request.id)
    failed_request_count += 1
    continue
except Exception:
    # This branch will catch any bubbled up exceptions not wrapped by
    # domain exceptions. This should not really happen as we should be
    # handling the possible error states.
    logger.exception("Unknown problem processing request %s", request.id)
    failed_request_count += 1
    continue
successful_request_count += 1
self._archive_if_done(request)
```

The imports at the top of `request_processor.py` must also include master's additions (`CustomerErasureAPI`, `CustomerErasureAPIException`, `ZendeskErasureAPI`, `ZendeskTicketId`, `ZendeskUserId`).

### 2. `template.yaml`

The conflict region is around the `ProcessFunction` SAM resource. Our branch added `AuditLogS3Bucket`/`AuditLogS3Prefix` parameters and the `ProcessS3WritePolicy` IAM resource. Master added the `CustomerErasuresApiUrl`, `ZendeskApiUrl`, `ZendeskApiToken` parameters and their env vars to `ProcessFunction`.

Resolution — **take both** additively: all parameters from both branches survive; `ProcessFunction.Environment.Variables` includes all env vars from both; `ProcessS3WritePolicy` (ours) and any new IAM resources from master both survive.

### 3. `tests/test_request_processor.py`

Two conflicts at the top:

**Conflict A — imports**
- `HEAD`: `from pytest import MonkeyPatch` + `AuditArchiver, NoOpAuditLogger`
- master: `from _pytest.logging import LogCaptureFixture` + `InMemoryAuditLogger, NoOpAuditLogger` + many new imports (`CustomerErasureAPI`, `ZendeskErasureAPI`, etc.)

Resolution — **take all**: `from pytest import MonkeyPatch` (ours) AND `from _pytest.logging import LogCaptureFixture` (theirs); `AuditArchiver` from ours; `InMemoryAuditLogger` and the new API imports from theirs.

**Conflict B — `ErasureState` import**
- `HEAD`: `from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import ErasureRequest, ErasureState`
- master: `from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import ErasureRequest` (no `ErasureState`)

Resolution — keep `ErasureState` (ours uses it for `_make_request(state: ErasureState)` helper) while also accepting master's new imports. Check if master's test additions use `ErasureState`; if not, keeping it is harmless.

The `_build_processor` factory helper in the test file also needs to be updated to pass the new required `erasures_api` and `zendesk` params when constructing `RequestProcessor` — both branches will have touched this.

### 4. `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`

Git reports auto-merged (no conflict markers). After merge, `processor_lambda.py` will include master's `customer_erasures_api`/`zendesk_api` wiring AND our archiver wiring. The `RequestProcessor(...)` call must pass all arguments: `request_repo, audit, as400, customer_api, customer_erasures_api, zendesk_api, dry_run=in_dry_run_mode, archiver=archiver`.

## Step-by-step implementation

1. `git merge origin/master` — starts the merge, leaves conflict markers.
2. Resolve `request_processor.py` — take both sides of both conflicts as described.
3. Resolve `template.yaml` — take all parameters and env vars from both sides.
4. Resolve `tests/test_request_processor.py` — take all imports from both sides; verify `_build_processor` passes `erasures_api` and `zendesk` to `RequestProcessor`.
5. Check `processor_lambda.py` auto-merge result — verify `RequestProcessor(...)` call includes all 8 arguments.
6. `uv run pytest -q` — full suite must be green.
7. `uv run basedpyright` — no new errors.
8. `git add` resolved files + `git commit` with a merge commit.
9. `git push origin DATA_4715_audit_log_to_s3`.

## Files modified

- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py` — conflict resolution (both sets of changes)
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py` — verify auto-merge; fix `RequestProcessor` call args
- `template.yaml` — conflict resolution (all parameters/env vars)
- `tests/test_request_processor.py` — conflict resolution (all imports; `_build_processor` factory args)

## Verification

1. `uv run pytest -q` — all tests green (expected ~220+ tests from both branches combined)
2. `uv run basedpyright` — 0 errors, 0 warnings
3. `sam validate --lint` — SAM template valid
4. `git log --oneline origin/master..HEAD` — shows our 11 branch commits + the merge commit on top

import os
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from typing import Any, Final, Literal

from aws_lambda_powertools import Logger
from aws_lambda_powertools.tracing import Tracer
from aws_lambda_powertools.utilities.typing import LambdaContext
from dotenv import load_dotenv

from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.as400 import AS400Notifier, NoOpAS400Notifier
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging import (
    AuditArchiver,
    AuditLogger,
    get_audit_log_archiver,
    get_audit_logger,
)
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.customer_api_client import (
    CustomerAPIClient,
    HTTPCustomerAPIClient,
)
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.customer_erasures_api_client import (
    CustomerErasureAPI,
    get_customer_erasures_api_from_env,
)
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.dryrun import is_dry_run
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_processor import RequestProcessor
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_repository import (
    RequestRepository,
    get_request_repository,
)
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.zendesk import ZendeskErasureAPI, get_zendesk_api_from_env

SAFETY_MARGIN_MS_ENV_VAR: Literal["PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS"] = (
    "PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS"
)
DEFAULT_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS: Literal[1000] = 1000

CUSTOMER_API_URL_ENV_VAR: Literal["CUSTOMER_API_URL"] = "CUSTOMER_API_URL"


def get_customer_api_url() -> str:
    """Get the Customer API URL from the environment, throwing a ValueError if not found"""
    url = os.getenv(CUSTOMER_API_URL_ENV_VAR)
    if url is None:
        raise ValueError(f"{CUSTOMER_API_URL_ENV_VAR} is not defined")
    return url


# Load .env file, mostly useful for testing
_ = load_dotenv()

logger = Logger()
tracer = Tracer()  # Sets service via POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME env var

customer_api_url: str = get_customer_api_url()
in_dry_run_mode: Final[bool] = is_dry_run()
request_repo: RequestRepository = get_request_repository()
audit: AuditLogger = get_audit_logger()
as400: AS400Notifier = NoOpAS400Notifier()  # TODO: add actual AS400 notifying
customer_api: CustomerAPIClient = HTTPCustomerAPIClient(customer_api_url)

try:
    archiver = get_audit_log_archiver()
except ValueError:
    logger.warning("Audit log archiver not configured; archival will be skipped")

processor: RequestProcessor = RequestProcessor(
    request_repo, audit, as400, customer_api, dry_run=in_dry_run_mode, archiver=archiver
=======

zendesk_api: ZendeskErasureAPI = get_zendesk_api_from_env()
processor: RequestProcessor = RequestProcessor(
    request_repo,
    audit,
    as400,
    customer_api,
    customer_erasures_api,
    zendesk_api,
    dry_run=in_dry_run_mode,
>>>>>>> origin/master
)


def get_safety_margin_ms() -> int:
    """Get the safety margin in milliseconds from the environment"""
    from_env = os.getenv("PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS")
    safety_margin_ms: int = DEFAULT_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS
    if from_env is not None:
        try:
            safety_margin_ms = int(from_env)
        except ValueError:
            logger.error(
                "Could not parse %s value %s as an integer",
                SAFETY_MARGIN_MS_ENV_VAR,
                from_env,
            )
    return safety_margin_ms


def get_timeout_datetime(remaining_millis: int) -> datetime:
    """Convert a remaining millis into a datetime"""
    delta = timedelta(milliseconds=remaining_millis)
    return datetime.now() + delta


@tracer.capture_lambda_handler
@logger.inject_lambda_context
def lambda_handler(
    _event: dict[Any, Any],  # pyright: ignore[reportExplicitAny]
    context: LambdaContext,
) -> None:
    """
    Entrypoint for AWS Lambda that does erasure request processing.
    """

    # Add a margin for safety so Lambdas don't timeout mid process
    safety_margin_ms: int = get_safety_margin_ms()

    remaining_millis = context.get_remaining_time_in_millis() - safety_margin_ms
    timeout: datetime = get_timeout_datetime(remaining_millis)
    logger.info(
        f"Processing events with a timeout of {remaining_millis}ms until {timeout}"
    )

    # Actual processing logic should be isolated in the processor class
    processor.process(timeout)

  ProcessS3WritePolicy:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Policy
    Properties:
      PolicyName: !Sub "rte-orchestrator-process-s3-write-${Environment}"
      PolicyDocument:
        Version: "2012-10-17"
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Action: s3:PutObject
            Resource: !Sub "arn:aws:s3:::${AuditLogS3Bucket}/${AuditLogS3Prefix}/*"
      Roles:
        - !GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleName

  ProcessSecretsSSMPermsPolicy:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Policy
    Properties:
      PolicyName: rte-orchestrator-processor-secret-perms
      PolicyDocument:
        Version: "2012-10-17"
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Action:
              - 'secretsmanager:DescribeSecret'
              - 'secretsmanager:GetSecretValue'
            Resource:
              - !Ref ZendeskAPIAuthSecret
              - !Ref CustomerErasuresAPIKeySecret
      Roles:
        - !GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleName
